chapters.title: Love and the myths that get in the way
chapters.text:
Picture coming home after a long day, hoping to see how much progress your spouse made on a cleaning task they had taken the day off to tackle. You open the door to find no chores done, and your partner is happily painting, surrounded by scattered art supplies.

That is what happened to Richard Pink and his wife, Roxanne, who had undiagnosed ADHD. Overwhelmed after a tough day, Richard snapped and asked how she could forget her promise and whether it was time to stop making excuses. His words brought Roxanne to the brink of tears, and both of them felt misunderstood.

Relationships are a bit like walking a tightrope, with mutual compassion and acceptance acting as the safety net.

Criticism can harm any relationship, but it is especially damaging when ADHD is involved. While most people get a mix of criticism and praise, those with undiagnosed ADHD usually feel constant shame. They are often called lazy or reckless, and they start to believe something is fundamentally wrong with them. Every shaming comment cuts deep.

So how can loved ones support someone with ADHD. What tips can ease the impact of the symptoms. Dive into Richard and Roxanne story to learn from their experience, find ways to strengthen your relationship, and turn ADHD into a source of connection rather than conflict.

chapters.title: The quiet power of empathy
chapters.text:
Have you ever put all your love into buying someone a gift, and they somehow lost it. Richard experienced that many times. Whenever Roxanne explained she had misplaced his present, Richard wondered whether she truly loved him.

For Richard, keeping things organized came naturally. So, if he lost something, he figured he had done it on purpose to show he did not care about the gift or the giver. He thought, if you truly value something, would you not protect it at all costs.

Yet, each time he saw Roxanne reaction to losing something, he could not believe it was intentional. She would beat herself up, turn red, and be on the verge of tears. Eventually, it dawned on him that he was reading her behavior through the lens of his own brain rather than hers. So, instead of judging Roxanne by his standards, Richard became curious about her perspective.

Empathy is the key to a person heart.

Feeling accepted for the first time, Roxanne shared that she had been struggling with losing things all her life. Apart from losing 18 wallets and 13 phones, she also lost a few door keys and once even slept on her doorstep. Each time she forgot something, she felt devastated and could not understand what was wrong with her.

These conversations helped them both feel understood, and soon the couple came up with solutions to stop losing possessions. They set up safety nets by leaving valuable items such as IDs at home whenever possible. They also bought phone insurance, made a special pocket for the phone, and kept extra money in the bag. They created a safe environment, so when Roxanne loses something nowadays, she feels free to call Richard to ease her stress. He also helps with practical steps, like canceling credit cards or calling restaurants to check if they found a lost item.

Compassion and a non-judgmental stance will bring about more positive change than shame and judgment ever will.

Although honest conversations helped the couple strengthen their bond, misconceptions continued until a game-changing moment, when Roxanne was diagnosed with ADHD. Suddenly, everything started to make sense.

chapters.title: Knowing the truth gives you power
chapters.text:
Words cannot describe how relieved Roxanne was when she found out she had ADHD. After decades of harsh self-criticism, eye rolls from others, and comments like just admit that you are lazy, she finally realized the real reason for her behavior.

She has ADHD, and her brain works a bit differently. That is why some tasks most people find easy, like making a bed, are challenging for her. At the same time, ADHD helps her excel in other areas that many find difficult, such as creative work. Her long-standing shame started to fade.

The diagnosis also gave her a way to explain her behavior. Now, she does not have to come up with excuses for losing things so often. She can simply tell others that it is a typical ADHD symptom and earn their understanding.

Knowing why something happens is half the work of fixing it.

The diagnosis was a relief for Richard too. Now, he understands why she acts the way she does and how to help her. Another ADHD symptom makes this clear, and that is time blindness.

Roxanne used to tell Richard she would be ready in ten minutes, but she would only start preparing half an hour later. It drove Richard crazy. He had never been late and could not understand how someone could be so unaware of time. Richard soon learned that people with ADHD tend to see time in two categories, right now and not now. The not now category can be five minutes or an hour, and to someone with ADHD, those periods feel the same. As a result, they are often late.

So, when Roxanne says she will be ready in ten minutes, Richard gently asks her with humor whether it will take ten minutes or ten ADHD minutes. He also checks in and reminds her of important events to reinforce her time awareness.

Roxanne also works on her time management, and a few tips she finds useful are these. Plan the route by getting as many details as possible. That helps her visualize the route and make more exact calculations. It also helps with another ADHD symptom, which is directional dyslexia, or struggling to find the way around. Warn others about potential lateness. Many people respond with understanding, which takes the pressure off and, in a strange twist, improves her chances of being on time.

Awareness of her ADHD symptoms has prompted Roxanne to make them work for her.

chapters.title: Speed can sometimes get in the way
chapters.text:
Impulsivity is probably the most well-known ADHD symptom, and for good reason. It can lead to life-changing decisions, like quitting a job or moving to another country, the kind of choices Roxanne used to make in her youth. As she got older, impulsivity started to shape her business decisions. She would try every new enterprise that caught her eye, buying domains and opening social media accounts. Each new business idea felt like her true purpose.

After learning about her diagnosis, Roxanne started to channel her impulsivity. She does not give up on her ideas but tests them using three filters. She checks the long-term perspective by asking herself whether she wants to spend the next ten years in this business. That helps her figure out which enterprises are fleeting obsessions and which are worth her time. For instance, she dropped the resin business idea but opened the ADHD_Love account, which went viral. She draws on self-knowledge by reflecting on childhood interests or asking parents and close friends about them. Often, the true purpose is something people naturally gravitate to without realizing it, and that is the activity worth focusing on. She also leans on Richard reality check. Richard never dismisses even Roxanne most outlandish ideas, since he knows how much she values them. At the same time, he always gently reminds her of the details and costs involved.

Impulsivity is not all bad. It can also spark genius ideas.

The next symptom, financial issues, depends heavily on impulsivity. Roxanne used to spend a lot, buying expensive items or giving others gifts she could not afford. Those habits left her in debt, and the cycle strengthened her belief that she was terrible with money. She feared opening bills and reading emails from the bank, certain she would not be able to sort things out.

As a bank manager, Richard had no fear of debts and helped Roxanne clear them one by one. He sat near her when she opened bills and explained that many issues could be sorted out quickly by email. After his crash course in budgeting basics, Roxanne reevaluated her attitude toward money. She also uses these pointers to minimize impulsive purchases. She practices patience by delaying each purchase to be sure she truly needs it. She also sets limits by avoiding credit cards to remove the temptation.

chapters.title: Focus can be a gift and a curse
chapters.text:
Some ADHD traits, like hyperfocus, can become part of a person identity, and that was true for Roxanne. As a child, she often spent hours reading about a topic, visiting related exhibitions, and talking endlessly about it.

Her enthusiasm led adults to label her a gifted child, assuming she had found her passion. However, it turned out to be hyperfocus, an intense but time-limited fixation on a subject she could rarely control. Eventually, Roxanne would lose interest and move on to another topic.

The gifted child label was Roxanne favorite part of herself, but it came with a cost, and that cost was high expectations. She felt obliged to succeed, so when she could not keep up with others in the usual tasks, she felt devastated as her whole identity came into question.

With support from her closest ones and therapy, Roxanne has befriended her high expectations and her hyperfocus. A few insights that helped her are these. She changed her self-evaluation, realizing she is gifted not because of any one skill or trait but because everyone is inherently talented in some way. She cultivates patience, since concentrating on something brings her joy, but acting on hyperfocus can lead her to abandon things she truly loves. So, she now waits before taking action, allowing time to reveal what matters. She asks for reminders. Richard usually shares Roxanne excitement, supports her research, and encourages her to take breaks to avoid burnout from long focus. At the same time, he often carves out specific time for hyperfocused research and reminds Roxanne about important events.

Avoid basing your identity on features that are out of your control.

Another ADHD symptom the couple tackles together is difficulty with object constancy. People with ADHD often struggle to remember things that are not in their immediate focus. For instance, Roxanne sometimes reads messages but does not reply for a long time.

This habit made Richard worry, with thoughts like is she alright or does she want to break up spinning in his head. So, the couple has come up with a few simple rules. Roxanne only reads messages when she has time to answer, and notifications serve as reminders. If Richard needs something urgent, he uses Roxanne preferred communication methods, such as phone and video calls.

chapters.title: A small pill for task avoidance
chapters.text:
As a person who cleaned carpets until vacuum lines were visible, Richard could not understand some of Roxanne habits. For example, he had never heard of a floordrobe, a pile of clothes on the floor, until one appeared on his freshly hoovered carpet. He also wondered why, after receiving messages about urgent tasks, Roxanne would do everything but those tasks.

After a while, he realized that this behavior is a classic example of task avoidance, closely linked to other ADHD symptoms like struggling with chores and poor personal hygiene.

Over time, Richard has noticed that Roxanne tends to avoid tasks that feel overwhelming. While cleaning a house seems straightforward to most, Roxanne has always struggled with it. Frustration led to avoidance, and a lack of practice only made the issue more daunting. She also tends to avoid tasks she finds boring. Roxanne used to swap showers for dry shampoo and put off washing clothes for weeks. She could not imagine how others had time for self-care when so many creative ventures were calling her name.

These insights helped Richard come up with several ways to support Roxanne, and a few of them are these. He makes her aware of task avoidance. Instead of saying just do it or stop wasting time, he gently reminds her that she seems to be getting interested in something else, and asks what she is up to. He adds novelty to tedious tasks. Unusual toothbrushes or bath oils can make self-care less boring for people with ADHD. He builds mutual routines. Brushing teeth or washing clothes together helps Roxanne feel less overwhelmed.

Roxanne has also come up with a few solutions of her own for tasks she does not feel like doing. She neutralizes overwhelming feelings by turning on her favorite music before they fully take hold, and she begins the day with priorities, knowing that postponing them will only add tension to her life. She works smart, not hard, on chores. Instead of writing long to-do lists, she starts with one daily task, like turning on the dishwasher or hanging up clothes. Seeing the result of her actions makes her more confident. She avoids perfectionism. Roxanne would love to sleep in clean pajamas after a shower and brushing her teeth, but that rarely happens. So, she has found a compromise in getting two out of three of those tasks done.

Celebrating even small progress helps change self-perception.

chapters.title: The other side of ADHD
chapters.text:
Although ADHD can make some tasks more challenging, it also brings several advantages. So, as Richard adds more organization to Roxanne life, she opens his world up to new opportunities and joys.

Consider others differences as a chance to notice your blind spots.

Richard, for instance, has always chosen the safest path. With Roxanne, he is more open to new, risky experiences that make his life more exciting and enjoyable. Risk-taking is just one advantage of people with ADHD, and others include these. Optimism. Despite many failed attempts, Roxanne never stopped trying new things, and she eventually found her passion in songwriting. Empathy. Years of feeling misunderstood taught Roxanne to recognize others emotions well. So, if something is wrong with Richard or their two children, Roxanne notices it right away and does everything she can to help them feel calm and safe. Richard greatly appreciates her care. Quick learner. If Roxanne is interested in something, her hyperfocus lets her pick up new skills quickly. Her research has also helped her family visit many stunning places they would never have found otherwise, and her curiosity is contagious, so the whole family learns something new daily. Sense of humor. Roxanne ability to turn mundane routines into joyful activities is extraordinary. Take brushing teeth as an example. With her funny dancing, the task brings everyone a lot of joy and laughter. Creativity. Brainstorming for Roxanne is a way of living. She can come up with 40 ideas for business names in a few minutes, and she regularly writes new songs. Her thinking out of the box is also excellent for problem-solving, since she quickly devises the fastest and easiest fix. Enjoyment of life. Richard did not have much money in his youth and got used to stretching every cent. Even after reaching financial success, he spent money only on necessities, ignoring many of the pleasures in life. Roxanne teaches him that money can enhance life, not just sustain it.

If I had to choose the most common sound that rings through our house, it would be laughter.

ADHD advantages flourish in a safe environment full of understanding, acceptance, and cooperation. Creating such an atmosphere takes time but is worthwhile.

A quick note. Walt Disney, Thomas Edison, and Albert Einstein all had ADHD.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
For years, Roxanne felt like a failure because of her differences. She often asked herself why she could not be like everyone else. But once she discovered that she had ADHD and accepted that her brain functions in its own unique way, she managed to turn her quirks into advantages.

Richard has also grown significantly, moving from projecting his views onto Roxanne to understanding events from her perspective. Their story proves that acceptance, empathy, and cooperation can help you and your loved ones complement each other and become an unstoppable team.

A small exercise to try. When talking with loved ones, try to look at the situation from their perspective. Write down your beliefs about your relationships and separate interpretations from facts. You can also gain a deeper understanding of ADHD and practical coping strategies by checking out the Headway summary of ADHD 2.0: New Science and Essential Strategies for Thriving with Distraction, from Childhood through Adulthood.
